title: Calculations using the ideal gas equation
description: Practice calculating pressure, volume, temperature, and moles of gas using the ideal gas equation
topic_path: Calculations using the ideal gas equation

assessment_items.question_content:
A $40.8\,\text{mol}$ sample of methane gas, $\text{CH}_4(g)$, is placed in a $1020\,\text L$ container at $298\,\text K$.

**What is the pressure, in $\text{atm}$, of the methane gas?**  
*Express the answer using 3 significant figures.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{atm}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
This is the ideal gas equation:

$\text{PV}=\text{nRT}$

We can rearrange the ideal gas equation to solve for the pressure of the methane. 

$\text{P}=\dfrac{\text{nRT}}{\text V}$

We can find the pressure of the methane gas by plugging in the values for the moles of gas, temperature, and volume. Since we want the pressure in units of $\text{atm}$, we use the $\text R$ value $0.082057\,\text{L atm K}^{-1}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{P}&=\dfrac{\text{nRT}}{\text V}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{(40.8\,\cancel{\text{mol}})(0.082057\,\dfrac{\cancel{\text L} \cdot \text{atm}}{\cancel{\text K} \cdot \cancel{\text{mol}}})(298\,\cancel{\text K})}{1020\,\cancel{\text L}}\\
\\
&=0.978\,\text{atm}
\end{align}$
The correct answer is $0.978\,\text{atm}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A $0.316\,\text{mol}$ sample of nitrogen gas, $\text{N}_2(g)$, is placed in a $4.00\,\text L$ container at $315\,\text K$.

**What is the pressure, in $\text{Torr}$, of the nitrogen gas?**  
*Express the answer using 3 significant figures.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{Torr}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
This is the ideal gas equation:

$\text{PV}=\text{nRT}$

We can rearrange the ideal gas equation to solve for the pressure of the nitrogen gas. 

$\text{P}=\dfrac{\text{nRT}}{\text V}$

We can find the pressure of the nitrogen gas by plugging in the values for the moles of gas, temperature, and volume. Since we want the pressure in units of $\text{Torr}$, we use the $\text R$ value $62.36358\,\text{L Torr K}^{-1}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{P}&=\dfrac{\text{nRT}}{\text V}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{(0.316\,\cancel{\text{mol}})(62.36358\,\dfrac{\cancel{\text L} \cdot \text{Torr}}{\cancel{\text K} \cdot \cancel{\text{mol}}})(315\,\cancel{\text K})}{4.00\,\cancel{\text L}}\\
\\
&=1550\,\text{Torr}
\end{align}$
The correct answer is $1550\,\text{Torr}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A balloon contains $1.82\,\text{mol}$ of oxygen gas, $\text O_2(g)$, at $303\,\text K$ and a pressure of $452\,\text{Torr}$. 

**What is the volume, in liters, of the balloon?**  
*Express the answer using 3 significant figures.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{L}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
This is the ideal gas equation:

$\text{PV}=\text{nRT}$

We can rearrange the ideal gas equation to solve for the volume of the oxygen gas. 

$\text{V}=\dfrac{\text{nRT}}{\text P}$

We can find the volume of the $\text O_2$ gas by plugging in the values for the moles of gas, temperature, and pressure. Since the pressure is in units of $\text{Torr}$, we use the $\text R$ value $62.36358\,\text{L Torr K}^{-1}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{V}&=\dfrac{\text{nRT}}{\text P}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{(1.82\,\cancel{\text{mol}})(62.36358\,\dfrac{\text L \cdot \cancel{\text{Torr}}}{\cancel{\text K} \cdot \cancel{\text{mol}}})(303\,\cancel{\text K})}{452\,\cancel{\text {Torr}}}\\
\\
&=76.1\,\text{L}
\end{align}$
The correct answer is $76.1\,\text{L}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A balloon contains $0.692\,\text{mol}$ of ammonia gas, $\text {NH}_3(g)$, at $280\,\text K$ and a pressure of $0.810\,\text{bar}$. 

**What is the volume, in liters, of the balloon?**  
*Express the answer using 3 significant figures.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{L}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
This is the ideal gas equation:

$\text{PV}=\text{nRT}$

We can rearrange the ideal gas equation to solve for the volume of the ammonia gas. 

$\text{V}=\dfrac{\text{nRT}}{\text P}$

We can find the volume of the $\text {NH}_3$ gas by plugging in the values for the moles of gas, temperature, and pressure. Since the pressure is in units of $\text{bar}$, we use the $\text R$ value $0.083145\,\text{L bar K}^{-1}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$.

$\begin{align}\text{V}&=\dfrac{\text{nRT}}{\text P}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{(0.692\,\cancel{\text{mol}})(0.083145\,\dfrac{\text L \cdot \cancel{\text{bar}}}{\cancel{\text K} \cdot \cancel{\text{mol}}})(280\,\cancel{\text K})}{0.810\,\cancel{\text {bar}}}\\
\\
&=19.9\,\text{L}
\end{align}$
The correct answer is $19.9\,\text{L}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A $0.0733\,\text{L}$ balloon contains $0.00230\,\text {mol}$ of $\text I_2$ vapor at a pressure of $0.924\,\text{atm}$.

**What is the temperature, in Kelvin, of the $\text I_2$ in the balloon?**  
*Express the answer using 3 significant figures.*

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text K$

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
This is the ideal gas equation:

$\text{PV}=\text{nRT}$

We can rearrange the ideal gas equation to solve for the temperature of the iodine gas. 

$\text{T}=\dfrac{\text{PV}}{\text {nR}}$

We can find the temperature of the $\text I_2$ gas by plugging in the values for the moles of gas, volume, and pressure. Since the pressure is in units of $\text{atm}$, we use the $\text R$ value $0.082057\,\text{L atm K}^{-1}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$. 

$\begin{align}\text{T}&=\dfrac{\text{PV}}{\text {nR}}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{(0.924\,\cancel{\text{atm}})(0.0733\,\cancel{\text{L})}}{(0.00230\,\cancel{\text {mol}})(0.082057\,\dfrac{\cancel{\text L} \cdot \cancel{\text{atm}}}{\cancel{\text{mol}}\cdot\text K})}\\
\\
&=359\,\text{K}
\end{align}$
The correct answer is $359\,\text{K}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A $2.37\,\text{L}$ balloon contains $0.115\,\text {mol}$ of xenon gas, $\text {Xe}(g)$, at a pressure of $954\,\text{Torr}$.

**What is the temperature, in Kelvin, of the $\text {Xe}$ in the balloon?**  
*Express the answer using 3 significant figures.*

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text K$

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
This is the ideal gas equation:

$\text{PV}=\text{nRT}$

We can rearrange the ideal gas equation to solve for the temperature of the xenon gas. 

$\text{T}=\dfrac{\text{PV}}{\text {nR}}$

We can find the temperature of the $\text {Xe}$ gas by plugging in the values for the moles of gas, volume, and pressure. Since the pressure is in units of $\text{Torr}$, we use the $\text R$ value $62.36358\,\text{L Torr K}^{-1}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$. 

$\begin{align}\text{T}&=\dfrac{\text{PV}}{\text {nR}}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{(954\,\cancel{\text{Torr}})(2.37\,\cancel{\text{L})}}{(0.115\,\cancel{\text {mol}})(62.36358\,\dfrac{\cancel{\text L} \cdot \cancel{\text{Torr}}}{\cancel{\text{mol}}\cdot\text K})}\\
\\
&=315\,\text{K}
\end{align}$
The correct answer is $315\,\text{K}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A sample of hydrogen gas, $\text{H}_2(g)$, is placed in a $0.500\,\text L$ container at $295\,\text K$. The gas pressure is $1.442\,\text{bar}$.

**How many moles of $\text H_2$ gas are in the container?**  
*Express the answer using 3 significant figures.*

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text {mol}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
This is the ideal gas equation:

$\text{PV}=\text{nRT}$

We can rearrange the ideal gas equation to solve for the moles of gas. 

$\text{n}=\dfrac{\text{PV}}{\text {RT}}$

We can find the moles of $\text H_2$ gas by plugging in the values for the temperature, volume, and pressure. Since the pressure is in units of $\text{bar}$, we use the $\text R$ value $0.083145\,\text{L bar K}^{-1}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$. 

$\begin{align}\text{n}&=\dfrac{\text{PV}}{\text {RT}}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{(1.442\,\cancel{\text{bar}})(0.500\,\cancel{\text{L})}}{(0.083145\,\dfrac{\cancel{\text L} \cdot \cancel{\text{bar}}}{\text{mol}\cdot \cancel{\text K}})
(295\,\cancel{\text K)}}\\
\\
&=0.0294\,\text{mol}\end{align}$
The correct answer is $0.0294\,\text{mol}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A sample of argon gas, $\text{Ar}(g)$, is placed in a $3.80\,\text L$ container at $320\,\text K$. The gas pressure is $0.496\,\text{atm}$.

**How many moles of $\text {Ar}$ gas are in the container?**  
*Express the answer using 3 significant figures.*

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text {mol}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
This is the ideal gas equation:

$\text{PV}=\text{nRT}$

We can rearrange the ideal gas equation to solve for the moles of gas. 

$\text{n}=\dfrac{\text{PV}}{\text {RT}}$

We can find the moles of $\text {Ar}$ gas by plugging in the values for the temperature, volume, and pressure. Since the pressure is in units of $\text{atm}$, we use the $\text R$ value $0.082057\,\text{L atm K}^{-1}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$. 

$\begin{align}\text{n}&=\dfrac{\text{PV}}{\text {RT}}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{(0.496\,\cancel{\text{atm}})(3.80\,\cancel{\text{L})}}{(0.082057\,\dfrac{\cancel{\text L} \cdot \cancel{\text{atm}}}{\text{mol}\cdot \cancel{\text K}})
(320\,\cancel{\text K)}}\\
\\
&=0.0718\,\text{mol}\end{align}$
The correct answer is $0.0718\,\text{mol}$.
